questions.prompt: Why is exec form `CMD ["node", "server.js"]` preferred over shell form `CMD node server.js`?

choices.text: Signal handling. Shell form is shorthand for `CMD ["/bin/sh", "-c", "node server.js"]` — your application runs as a child of /bin/sh, not as PID 1 in the container. When Docker sends SIGTERM (during `docker stop`, orchestrator scale-down, or rolling update), it goes to the shell, which often doesn't forward it to children. Your app doesn't get SIGTERM; it doesn't get a chance to gracefully shut down; eventually SIGKILL after the grace period drops in-flight requests. Exec form runs the binary directly as PID 1; signals reach the app; graceful shutdown works. Same issue applies to ENTRYPOINT — use exec form. The shell-form vs exec-form distinction is one of the most common sources of 'why does my container drop requests on shutdown' bugs. Universal best practice: exec form for production.

questions.explanation: Exec form runs the binary as PID 1 directly; signals reach it. Shell form wraps in /bin/sh, breaking signal forwarding. Always exec form in production.

questions.prompt: A Dockerfile has `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl` followed by `RUN rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`. Image is still bloated. Why?

choices.text: The cleanup is in a separate RUN, so it creates a new layer that hides /var/lib/apt/lists/* but doesn't remove it from the layer where apt-get install added it. Layers are immutable; deleting in a later layer creates a whiteout. The apt cache files still take space in the earlier layer; the final image size is the sum of all layers. Fix: combine into a single RUN: `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`. The single RUN's final layer doesn't include the apt cache (deleted within the layer), so image size reflects the actual end state. This pattern applies to any 'install + clean up' operations: combine into one RUN. Common offenders: pip caches (`--no-cache-dir` or rm), npm caches (`npm cache clean --force && rm -rf ~/.npm`), apt lists. Combining cleanup with install is one of the highest-impact Dockerfile optimizations.

questions.explanation: Cleanup must be in the same RUN as the install. Layers are immutable; later-layer deletions don't reduce earlier-layer sizes.
